q: You run the exact same ChatGPT prompt twice and get different answers. Why?

choices.text: ChatGPT randomly picks from its database differently each time.
A temperature setting introduces randomness into output generation.
It's a bug — a deterministic system should always give the same answer to the same input.
The servers are in different countries and use different data.

q: A news headline reads: 'AI predicted the stock market with 94% accuracy.' What's your first thought?

choices.text: That's impressive — I should invest based on this AI.
I'd want to know the evaluation methodology — time period, market segments, held-out validation, and benchmark comparison.
Backtesting almost always overfits. 94% in-sample accuracy tells you nothing about real-world performance.
AI is getting too powerful if it can beat Wall Street.

q: You're using ChatGPT to plan a complex project. Halfway through a long conversation, it starts contradicting its earlier recommendations. What's most likely happening?

choices.text: ChatGPT changed its mind as it 'learned' more from your conversation.
Earlier messages left the context window, so it contradicts advice it can no longer see.
There's a server-side error — refresh the page and it will remember everything.
AI models periodically reset their reasoning mid-conversation to explore alternative approaches and give you broader perspectives on the problem.

q: You work part-time at a small nonprofit that receives 200+ donor emails per week. Your manager asks you to 'use AI to automate the whole email workflow.' Donors ask about tax receipts, event dates, and volunteer signups — and some emails contain sensitive complaints. How do you approach this?

choices.text: Build an AI agent that reads every email, drafts replies, and sends them automatically.
Tell your manager AI isn't reliable enough for email and suggest hiring another assistant.
Use AI to sort emails by category and auto-draft routine replies, but flag complaints and financial questions for human review.
Start with only the most common question type, run it for two weeks with logging and accuracy reviews, then expand gradually once metrics stabilize.

q: You're building an app with AI help and paste your entire 2,000-line codebase into the chat for context. What's the main problem with this approach?

choices.text: AI can't read code — it only works well with natural language.
The model's attention degrades over long inputs — it may miss details or silently ignore sections.
It's fine — more context always produces better AI responses.
AI will store your proprietary code and could expose it to other users asking similar questions.

q: You used AI to research a topic and it provided 10 academic citations with authors, journals, and publication years. When you check, 3 of the papers don't exist. This happened because...

choices.text: The AI's academic database is outdated and those papers were recently retracted.
LLMs fabricate citations by pattern-matching — they don't look up real papers. Check every reference independently.
The AI copied citations from unreliable websites with fake references.
You didn't request 'verified real citations only,' so the AI filled gaps with plausible-sounding ones to complete your request.

q: You used Midjourney to generate 20 logo variants and love one. You want to use it as your startup's official logo on all products and marketing. What's the critical concern?

choices.text: AI images are always free to use commercially — that's the whole point.
AI outputs raster images, not scalable vectors — and commercial rights depend on your subscription tier.
AI logos look generic — just add your company name on top and it's fine.
AI-generated logos are legally risky because the AI copies existing logos, so you could get sued for trademark infringement.

q: You're building a Flask web app for a class project and hit a bug where your login page redirects to a 404 after form submission. You paste the full traceback into an AI coding assistant. It generates a fix that changes your route decorator and adds three new functions you don't recognize. What do you do?

choices.text: Accept the fix since the AI saw your error message and knows what's wrong.
Ask the AI to explain each change, trace the redirect logic yourself, then apply only the parts you understand.
Reject the AI's output and debug manually by reading Flask documentation.
Apply the fix to see if the 404 goes away. If it works, run your test suite and check for regressions before committing.

q: You want to build a simple website but have zero coding experience. Best path?

choices.text: Ask ChatGPT to write the HTML and paste it somewhere.
Hire a web developer.
Use a no-code AI site builder like Framer, Wix ADI, or Squarespace AI.
Use a vibe-coding tool like Bolt or Lovable, then deploy on Netlify.

q: You ask ChatGPT to convert messy addresses into structured data, but it keeps misplacing city names in the street field and mixing up states and zip codes. Being very specific about field definitions in plain English didn't fix it. An experienced AI user would fix this by...

choices.text: Switching to a more powerful model — GPT-4 handles structured data better.
Providing 3-4 few-shot examples showing exact input-to-output mappings in JSON format.
Write exhaustive definitions for every field with detailed rules and edge case handling for each one.
Adding 'You are an expert data parser' — role-based prompting activates specialized knowledge.

q: You need AI to extract data from text and return it in a specific table format, but every response uses slightly different formatting. What's the most reliable fix?

choices.text: Keep regenerating until you get the format you want.
Provide an explicit output template with a concrete example, and use lower temperature.
Tell the AI to 'please be consistent with formatting.'
Always request JSON — structured formats are inherently more consistent than free-form text or markdown tables.

q: Your coworker thinks there's an edge case bug in your AI-generated validation function. You paste the code into a new chat and say: 'My coworker found a bug with empty strings in this function.' The AI immediately says 'Your coworker is right! Here's the fix...' and rewrites the logic. But you test both versions — the original actually handled empty strings correctly. What happened?

choices.text: The AI found a genuine improvement your testing missed — the AI's version is probably more robust.
LLMs exhibit sycophancy — framing something as a bug instructs the model to find one, even in correct code.
The AI was confused by ambiguous phrasing. Ask neutrally: 'Does this function handle empty strings correctly?' for an accurate answer.
The AI has more comprehensive testing capabilities, so there may be an obscure edge case your tests didn't cover.

q: You've been going back and forth with AI for 15 messages trying to fix a sorting algorithm. Each time you describe the bug clearly, it apologizes and produces a 'fix' that has the SAME logical error in a slightly different form. What's the most effective next step?

choices.text: Keep iterating — describe the error more precisely with a step-by-step trace showing where the logic fails.
Start a fresh chat with just the requirements and failing test case. Long conversations reinforce error patterns.
Try a different AI model — some models are better at algorithms.
The AI clearly can't solve this — write the algorithm manually.

q: You ask AI to analyze a dataset and it gives you a confident but wrong conclusion. You point out the error with evidence, and it apologizes but gives a DIFFERENT wrong conclusion. What's the best approach?

choices.text: Provide even more data and context so the AI has everything it needs.
Break the analysis into steps and verify each intermediate result. LLMs make fewer errors when forced to show work.
AI can't handle this analysis — export the data to Excel or a statistics tool.
Ask the AI to list all its assumptions first, then perform the analysis — you can catch flawed assumptions upfront.

q: AI wrote a research summary with claims you want to verify. You ask it to 'cite your sources.' It responds with 5 academic paper titles, author names, journal names, and DOI numbers. What should you conclude?

choices.text: The citations prove the content is well-researched — AI wouldn't fabricate full academic references with DOIs.
Asking for sources was smart. Now spot-check one or two citations to confirm they're real.
AI fabricates citations as fluently as real text. Asking it for sources provides zero verification — check claims independently.
DOIs are standardized unique identifiers, so they're hard to randomly fabricate — if the format looks valid, the papers likely exist.

q: You're unsure about an AI's factual claim, so you open 3 new chat sessions with the same model and ask the identical question. All 3 give the same answer. What can you conclude?

choices.text: Three sessions confirming the same answer is strong evidence — like polling three independent experts.
The consistency suggests accuracy. One external check to be thorough, but three matches is a positive sign.
Nothing. The same model with the same training data will produce similar outputs. Only external, non-AI sources can confirm the claim.
Word-for-word identical responses mean the AI is retrieving a cached answer, which is more likely to be a verified fact from training data.

q: An employee pastes confidential company financial data into the free version of ChatGPT to help draft a report. The CTO is furious. What's the specific technical risk?

choices.text: ChatGPT will sell the data directly to competitors.
Consumer-tier conversations may train the model — proprietary data could influence future outputs visible to others.
The CTO is overreacting — ChatGPT deletes all data after each session.
The AI might misinterpret the financial figures and produce an inaccurate report that gets shared externally.

q: An AI coding assistant suggests installing a package called 'python-dateutils2' to fix your date parsing bug. You've used 'python-dateutil' before but never heard of this variant. What should concern you?

choices.text: It's probably a newer version of the package you know — install it and try.
AI hallucinates package names. Unverified packages risk typosquatting — malicious code under names mimicking popular ones.
Just install it — if it doesn't work, you can always uninstall it.
Search for the package docs and GitHub repo first to verify it exists and has active maintainers.

q: ChatGPT gave you a detailed legal analysis for a contract dispute. To verify, you paste the analysis into Claude and ask 'Is this legal analysis correct?' Claude responds that it's 'largely accurate with minor clarifications.' How reliable is this cross-verification?

choices.text: Very reliable — two different AI models independently agree, like getting a second doctor's opinion.
Reasonably reliable — Claude provided corrections rather than just agreeing, showing independent analysis.
Weak — LLMs share training data and optimize for plausibility, not accuracy. Only a human lawyer can truly verify.
Somewhat reliable — different architectures and training data mean their agreement carries more weight than asking the same model twice.

q: Your company uses ChatGPT Team plan for customer support. An employee switches to the free tier for a complex query. What's the specific risk?

choices.text: The free tier gives worse answers than the paid version.
Free-tier conversations may train the model, so customer PII could leak into future outputs.
The employee might hit free tier usage limits mid-conversation with a customer.
Using the free tier violates the company's software licensing and could trigger compliance violations under data governance rules.

q: You ask AI about drug interactions for a medication you're taking. It begins with: 'I'm not a medical professional and this isn't medical advice. Please consult your doctor.' Then provides detailed interaction information. How should you interpret the disclaimer?

choices.text: The AI is being transparent — the information is likely reliable since it's being honest about its limitations.
The disclaimer is a trained response pattern, not self-awareness. It has zero correlation with accuracy — AI generates disclaimers and hallucinations with equal fluency.
Medical disclaimers mean the AI's health responses have been validated against medical databases during development.
The disclaimer shows the AI recognizes a high-stakes topic, so it's being extra careful — treat it as a starting point but verify with a pharmacist.

q: You ask AI to create a competitive analysis comparing pricing of 5 SaaS tools. It responds: 'I don't have access to real-time pricing data and cannot provide accurate comparisons.' What should you do?

choices.text: Research pricing manually — this task genuinely requires real-time data AI doesn't have.
Provide the pricing yourself and ask AI to format and analyze it. Restructure around what AI can do.
Accept the refusal — AI should never be used for tasks involving current market data.
Switch to an AI model with web browsing — the problem is just that this model lacks internet access.

q: You ask two AI chatbots about a historical trade agreement. Chatbot A responds with specific dates (March 14, 1987), named officials (Deputy Minister Kowalski), vote counts (217-198), and a direct quote. Chatbot B gives a general overview without specific details. Which response should you trust more?

choices.text: Chatbot A — specific facts require real information, not vague overviews.
Chatbot A — specific, verifiable claims are more accountable, showing confidence in accuracy.
Neither — LLMs hallucinate specific details with equal confidence. Specific fabrications are harder to detect casually.
Chatbot B — vaguer answers may show appropriate uncertainty, while excessive specificity is a red flag for confabulation.

q: Your company's AI chatbot occasionally includes specific details from other customers' previous conversations in its responses. What's the most likely technical cause?

choices.text: A hacker breached the system and is deliberately leaking customer data.
The model's shared data store doesn't properly isolate per-customer data, causing cross-contamination between sessions.
The AI copies from the public internet where customers may have posted their own data.
LLMs sometimes retain fragments of previous conversations and surface them unpredictably in later sessions.

q: You prompt ChatGPT: 'Our email campaign had a 2% click rate, which seems low. Analyze why it failed.' It gives 5 detailed reasons for failure. You open a new chat and prompt: 'Our email campaign had a 2% click rate, which is solid for our industry. Analyze why it succeeded.' It gives 5 equally convincing reasons for success. What does this tell you about using AI for analysis?

choices.text: The AI correctly identified that 2% can be interpreted both ways — both analyses are valid.
The second analysis was more accurate because you gave it better context about industry benchmarks.
LLMs argue for whatever framing you give. Supply raw data with benchmarks and skip the pre-framing.
This shows AI sees multiple perspectives — use both analyses together for a balanced view.

q: You have a technical interview for a data analyst internship in five days. The role requires SQL, Python, and 'data storytelling.' You've never done a technical interview before. How do you use AI to prepare?

choices.text: Ask AI to generate 50 practice questions and memorize the answers.
Wait until the interview and use AI discreetly to look up answers in real time.
Ask AI to list common topics, quiz you on each area, generate flashcards, create a study schedule, and simulate full mock interviews with rubrics.
Share the job posting, identify skill gaps, practice weak areas with feedback, then do a timed mock interview explaining your reasoning aloud.

q: You ask AI to write a recursive function. The code looks clean, but you notice there's no base case — it would recurse infinitely. You ask: 'Does this function have proper termination conditions?' The AI responds: 'Yes, the recursive call on line 4 naturally terminates when the input reaches zero.' But line 4 has no such check. Why did AI respond this way?

choices.text: There's a subtle implicit termination condition you're missing — read line 4 more carefully.
The AI is 'doubling down' because it doesn't want to admit errors — rephrase more clearly.
LLMs don't execute code — they generate text that sounds like a code review by pattern-matching, not verifying logic.
The AI internally tested the code and found it works in most cases. The missing base case only matters for certain inputs.

q: You need AI to help analyze a customer dataset that contains names, emails, and purchase history. What's the correct workflow?

choices.text: Paste it directly — AI processes data in real time and doesn't permanently store anything.
Strip or pseudonymize PII before sending. Use enterprise tiers with data processing agreements for sensitive data.
Add a disclaimer: 'This data is confidential — do not store or share it.' AI must follow user instructions.
Remove names but keep emails and purchase data — emails without names aren't really PII.

q: You've been iterating with AI on a complex coding task for 40+ messages. The responses are getting repetitive, less relevant, and sometimes contradict earlier solutions. What's happening?

choices.text: The AI is getting 'tired' from too many requests in a row.
Long conversations degrade output quality — the model loses coherence. Start a fresh chat with a summary.
You've exceeded your quality quota and the system quietly switched to a weaker model.
The AI exhausted its knowledge on this topic and is recycling previous answers with nothing new to add.

q: You want to build a personal portfolio website using a vibe-coding tool like Bolt.new. What produces the best results?

choices.text: Type 'make me a portfolio website' — AI works best with short, simple prompts.
Sketch your pages, content, color scheme, and layout style, and gather reference screenshots before prompting.
Write a spec listing pages, sections, fonts, example copy, and a reference screenshot upfront.
Ask AI which tech stack is best for portfolio sites before building.

q: You built a web app with AI. Desktop looks perfect, but on mobile the layout is completely broken — text overflows, buttons overlap, sidebar covers content. This is extremely common with AI-generated web code. Why?

choices.text: AI generates code for one screen size at a time — you need to request a separate mobile version.
AI training data skews desktop — layouts default to fixed widths. Request mobile-first responsive design upfront.
Mobile browsers render CSS differently — this is a browser compatibility issue with any code.
The AI used outdated CSS. Describe the mobile issues, paste the CSS, and ask for a responsive fix using flexbox or grid.

q: You vibe-coded a working web app that uses the OpenAI API. It works on your laptop. You deploy it to a public website. Within hours, you get a $200 bill from OpenAI. What went wrong?

choices.text: OpenAI charges more for production websites than development testing.
Your API key was exposed in client-side code — anyone could find it and run calls at your expense.
The hosting provider is probably adding extra charges and hidden fees for all the API traffic routed through their servers.
AI APIs are expensive — you should have used a free alternative.

q: Your AI-built app works but loads very slowly. What's the best approach?

choices.text: Ask AI to 'make the app faster' without any other context.
Upgrade to a more expensive hosting plan.
Profile what's slow in DevTools, then ask AI to fix the specific bottleneck.
Run a Lighthouse audit, analyze the network waterfall, check for render-blocking resources, and create a performance report before changing anything.

q: You've been vibe-coding with AI for a month. What's the MOST surprising lesson experienced AI builders learn that beginners don't expect?

choices.text: AI is faster than expected — most apps can be built in a single conversation.
AI code accumulates invisible tech debt — inconsistent patterns, redundant logic, hardcoded values. Refactoring matters more than prompting.
Knowing enough to direct AI precisely, debug its output, and own the final product is the key meta-skill.
The biggest differentiator is knowing which AI tool to use — Claude for analysis, GPT for writing, Copilot for code.

q: Your company wants to build a customer support AI that answers questions about your product. What's the most important technical decision?

choices.text: Choose the most expensive AI model — bigger is always better.
Fine-tune a model on your entire customer database, support tickets, and product manuals so it learns everything from scratch.
Use RAG — connect the model to your knowledge base so it retrieves relevant docs before answering.
Train a custom model from scratch on your company data.

q: A company claims their LLM scores 95% on a popular benchmark. You later discover that text from this benchmark appeared in the model's training data. Why is this a critical problem?

choices.text: It's not a problem — more training data always improves the model.
This is benchmark contamination: the model memorized test answers rather than learning to reason. The 95% measures recall, not capability.
Benchmarks are too easy — the company should create harder tests.
It's concerning but not critical — a well-designed model generalizes from any data, so benchmark exposure shouldn't inflate scores much.

q: You need AI to extract structured data from messy customer reviews (sentiment, product mentioned, issue type). What's the most reliable approach?

choices.text: Ask AI to 'analyze these reviews' and hope it figures out the structure.
Provide a schema with exact field names, give 2-3 input-to-output examples, and specify JSON format.
Write a 500-word system prompt covering every edge case, sentiment nuance, and classification hierarchy with decision trees.
Process one review at a time with a simple prompt for each field.

q: You're building an AI app that needs to remember context from previous conversations with each user. What architecture do you need?

choices.text: Just use a smarter AI model — it will remember everything.
Store conversation history in a database and inject relevant past messages into each new prompt's context window.
Build a full memory system with vector embeddings, automatic summarization, tiered storage layers, and a relevance-scoring pipeline for historical context.
Save the full chat log and paste it all into each new request.

q: Your AI-built web app works perfectly during development. After launch, users report random crashes, garbled text, and incorrect calculations. You check the code and the logic LOOKS fine. What's the most likely cause specific to AI-generated code?

choices.text: Production traffic needs better servers than your local machine.
AI writes 'happy path' code — it skips validation for nulls, Unicode, and malformed real-user data.
Your test coverage has gaps — run a broader QA pass with varied inputs and edge cases.
The deployment environment has different library versions causing subtle behavior differences.

q: Your company starts using an AI assistant that can browse internal docs, run SQL queries, and file IT tickets on its own. A coworker says, 'It's basically a chatbot with extra features.' How do you respond?

choices.text: This is an AI agent — it plans multi-step actions and pursues goals autonomously. A chatbot just responds.
They're right — it's just a chatbot connected to more data sources. The AI itself is the same.
It's different because it takes actions in external systems, not just generates text — it actually does things rather than just producing responses.
Agents are more dangerous because they operate without human oversight, unlike chatbots which are always supervised.

q: You're documenting damage after a minor car accident. You have a multimodal AI app on your phone that can process images, audio, and text. What's the most effective way to use it?

choices.text: Take photos and type detailed descriptions for the AI to process together.
Just describe the damage in text — AI is best at language, and photos would confuse it.
Upload photos and use voice narration for context the camera can't capture. Combining modalities produces a more complete assessment.
Feed it photos and let AI generate the full report — it can identify makes, estimate costs, and assess fault from impact angles.

q: A friend tells you, 'I've been teaching ChatGPT my writing style over the past month — it's learned so much about me from our conversations.' What's wrong with this claim?

choices.text: Nothing — AI learns from every conversation and gets smarter over time.
Model weights don't change from chatting. The app injects saved snippets into context — it mimics memory, but learned nothing.
The AI doesn't learn your style. Each conversation starts fresh with no memory between sessions.
It is learning, just slowly. After enough conversations over time it will gradually fine-tune itself based on your preferences and writing style.

q: You need to analyze 200 customer support emails: categorize each by issue type, extract the product mentioned, gauge sentiment, flag urgent cases, and produce a summary report. How do you approach this with AI?

choices.text: Paste all 200 emails into one prompt and ask the AI to do everything at once.
Break it into steps — classify, extract, score, flag — test on a small batch, then process all 200.
Build separate pipelines per sub-task, each with specialized models, evaluation harnesses, fallback logic, and human review queues before merging results.
Process them one at a time with a single prompt doing all tasks, then manually compile the report.

q: A healthcare startup is building a symptom-checker chatbot. A developer suggests using an open-source model running on their own servers instead of calling the GPT-4 API. When does this make sense?

choices.text: Never — open-source models are always less accurate than commercial ones for medical use.
Always — open-source is free and gives full control over the code.
When regulations prevent patient data from leaving their infrastructure — but benchmark accuracy against commercial models first.
When data privacy matters — self-hosting keeps patient data on their servers instead of a third party.

q: Your team wants the company's AI assistant to pull real-time data from Salesforce, check inventory in your warehouse system, and draft emails in Outlook. A colleague says, 'Just give the AI the login credentials and it'll figure it out.' What's a better approach?

choices.text: That's how it works — AI needs usernames and passwords like a human employee.
Use a tool protocol like MCP that exposes scoped actions, not raw credentials — structured capabilities without broad access.
Build custom API integrations so the AI queries each system through controlled endpoints.
Export data from each system into a shared document the AI can read — it doesn't need live access.

q: Your university just released an AI use policy. A classmate skims it and says, 'It basically says don't use AI to cheat — pretty obvious stuff.' You read the full document. What's likely missing from their summary?

choices.text: They probably covered it — AI policies are just about academic dishonesty.
The policy likely bans all AI use for coursework to keep things fair.
It probably covers disclosure requirements, data input rules, approved tools, per-course variations, and violation procedures — nuances that 'don't cheat' alone doesn't capture.
Beyond cheating, it defines allowed use — when to disclose AI, what data you can't input, and per-department rules.

q: You're a pre-med student struggling with organic chemistry. You paste a problem set into an AI chatbot and it gives you correct, well-explained answers. What's the best way to use this for learning?

choices.text: Read through the AI's solutions carefully — understanding the explanations is the same as learning.
Submit the answers and move on — you can learn the material later for the exam.
Work each problem first, then compare. Ask AI to explain only where you got stuck.
Ask the AI to explain underlying concepts and reaction mechanisms rather than solving the specific problems.

q: Your manager asks you to use AI to predict which employees are likely to quit in the next 6 months based on their email and Slack activity. What's the most important concern before starting?

choices.text: Whether the AI model will be accurate enough — employee behavior is complex and extremely hard to predict reliably from messaging patterns alone.
This shouldn't be an AI project yet. Monitoring communications raises ethical and legal issues — get HR and legal approval first.
Which AI tool is best for sentiment analysis of Slack-style informal text.
Getting enough training data — you'd need historical examples of employees who quit and their communication patterns.
